What happens: Leo pressures his friend Ian Hagen into taking on Jay Hall as an assistant, reopening the wound of Ian's betrayal by the corporate spy Sam Connelly. Ian reluctantly agrees that Jay will start "Monday morning."
Note: No explicit date in text; "the following Monday" places this the prior week (the "couple of days before" the start).

What happens: Jay arrives early at Northlight Financial Center for his first day and is welcomed by Leo, who shows him to the desk outside Ian's office. Jay is privately relieved to have landed under a boss famously immune to office romance.
Milestone: Jay's first day at Turner & Associates

What happens: Ian emerges, sizes Jay up with suspicion, and introduces him to the team (Amy, Luc, Ela, Zoe, Kim). Jay's earnest competence reads to Ian as exactly what a skilled manipulator would project.
Characters: Ian, Jay, Amy, Ela, Luc, Kim, Zoe

What happens: Over the next two mornings Jay learns the espresso machine from Luc and receives the leather messenger bag — handed over openly by Leo at the office. Ian works the early Tokyo-deal calls and keeps watching Jay through the glass.

By Friday Jay has finished a strong first week of progressively harder tasks, delighted by the respectful, hands-off environment. Ian reads Jay's obvious happiness as the same slow-build charm Sam once used, and resolves to stay guarded.

Jay spends his first weekend off in disciplined solitude — studying client files, batch-cooking, ironing his seven identical shirts. A brief text from his mother underscores how isolated his life has become.

Week two opens with Ian instituting standing ~9 a.m. briefings and assigning the Maven Holdings summary (due end of week, for the following Monday's board meeting). Jay reluctantly joins the team lunch at Cornerstone Cafe and finds the social performance exhausting.

Daily lunch invitations quietly absorb Jay into the group; over Thursday's lunch Ela notes Max is due back "week after next." Late Thursday Jay finds a transposition error in his portfolio projections, and Ian — instead of scolding — sits down and walks him through the fix.
Jay submits a strong investment analysis that Ian praises, then declines the Friday Murphy's drinks invitation with an errand excuse. He heads home content, preferring solitude to the bar.

That evening Ian and Leo drink scotch in the office while Leo needles Ian about his paranoia and Jay's evident loneliness. Ian refuses to soften, telling himself some lessons are too expensive to learn twice.
Now "almost three weeks" in, Jay discovers the Willow & Sage bistro and a quiet pocket park near the office. Friday brings Ela's news that Max's return is delayed again by a tropical illness.

Starting his fourth week, Jay is pulled into a Thursday strategy session on the Wentworth account and correctly reframes the client's emails as wanting reassurance, not a strategy change. Ian and Amy both acknowledge the insight.
Milestone: Jay's first substantive contribution (Wentworth) — Thursday, August 25

What happens: Jay finally joins Friday drinks, nursing one beer and leaving by nine-thirty, but surviving the social test. Walking to the metro, Luc hints obliquely that he too was one of Leo's unusual hires.
Milestone: Jay's first after-work outing with colleagues

What happens: Comfortable in the office's routines, Jay watches Ian quietly praise good work across the team on Thursday, cementing his admiration. Luc explains Leo's real status as company royalty.

Jay's first month-end pay lands with an unexpected standard monthly bonus — nearly triple his old paycheck — and Luc confirms it's routine. Jay celebrates quietly with coffee and a croissant, then banks most of it, mindful that two months of probation remain.
Milestone: First month complete; first salary + bonus

What happens: A month in, Ian privately admits his attraction to and respect for Jay — the exact combination that once destroyed him — even as Leo delivers a spotless background check. Ian notes that Jay's probation ends "at the beginning of November" and recommits to vigilance.

Emboldened by a good discussion, Jay asks about the old Falconridge acquisition and Ian shuts him down coldly. Jay, mortified, retreats into deliberate invisibility while Ian's manner toward him turns glacial.

Kim notes Jay's new silence; on Thursday Jay stays until 9:30 to find the decimal error in Amy's Broadmarsh reconciliation. The team's gratitude only deepens his conviction that he belongs in the background.
Note: Overlaps Scene 2's aftermath.

What happens: Max Goodwin returns from his extended trip and, loudly oblivious, greets Jay as "Leo's new joke hire." That night Jay searches Ian's name, finds the old corporate-espionage scandal and the gossip about Ian's "type," and feels his safe harbor turn false.
Milestone: Max returns; "joke hire" revelation

What happens: Numb and withdrawn, Jay moves through his duties as pure performance while the quarterly reports ramp up with September ending. Checking his bank balance after two paychecks, he frames his security as a transaction — dignity traded for safety.
Milestone: Q3 quarterly reporting begins

What happens: As the first week of October closes, Jay attends his first lavish quarterly party in a costly midnight-blue suit, feeling like an impostor while Ian gives him only fifteen seconds of cold courtesy. Leo teases Ian for watching Jay all night and fumbling the chance to help him.
Milestone: First quarterly party

What happens: To signal that Jay is valued, Ian assigns him the sensitive Stormfront Group confidentiality audit, normally his own task. Jay delivers exceptional work the following Thursday, disarming — but not erasing — Ian's suspicion.
Milestone: Stormfront compliance audit delivered (Thursday, October 20)

What happens: After nearly two weeks of war-room effort, Ian personally lands the prestigious Hartwell account, confirmed on a Friday afternoon. At the debrief Max jokes that Jay's "pretty face" helped close it, souring the team's genuine praise of his coordination work.
Milestone: Hartwell acquisition signed
Note: Runs concurrently with Scene 2 — see flags.

What happens: Jay keeps declining the now-ceremonial Friday drinks; the following Tuesday Max privately apologizes for the "joke hire" remark. With his probation a week off, Jay half-hopes the company won't renew him, so he can leave with his dignity.

At the 2 p.m. three-month review, Ian praises Jay's work, offers permanent employment (10% raise, three-year contract, benefits), and comes as close as he can to apologizing for his coldness. Jay accepts flatly — secure at last, but hollowed out, with success feeling indistinguishable from survival.
Milestone: Probation ends; permanent contract offered — end of Arc 1

What happens: The team gathers at Murphy's to celebrate Jay's permanent status, and Ian makes a rare appearance to mark it. Pulling Leo aside, Ian coldly orders him to repair the damage his "joke hire" scheme did to Jay's confidence.
Milestone: Celebration of Jay's permanent contract; Ian's rare social appearance

What happens: Leo insists on driving Jay home near midnight and comes up to explain himself: appearance was a factor in the hire, but Jay has earned his place entirely on merit. Jay is left with answers but lingering unease.

Alone with whiskey and a private investigator's report, Ian admits he has been watching Jay out of concern, not suspicion, and that Leo was right about his innocence. He resolves to become the fair mentor Jay needs — while forbidding himself anything more.

The office feels reset; Ian praises Jay's work and, on Tuesday, brings him into a Mayfield client meeting to observe and take notes. Jay begins to believe the office is simply what it appears to be — civil people doing good work.

A Fairfield Industries crisis — their board meeting moved up — keeps Jay and Ian working alone past eleven; Ian patiently helps Jay untangle a backdated-acquisition discrepancy. Jay realizes that here, at least, he can stop flinching.
Milestone: Fairfield Industries late-night crisis

What happens: The Fairfield presentation succeeds without a single revision and Ian gives the team the afternoon off; Jay spends it reading at Café Lemon. On Saturday he finally calls his mother, sharing his stable new life.

Ian emails a formal invitation to a Friday celebration dinner at The Palladium for the Hartwell success, with Turner Senior and Leo attending. Over lunch the team trades stories that show Jay a drier, more human side of Ian.

The Hartwell workload ramps up with a Tuesday briefing while talk turns to Friday's dinner. The Palladium evening goes far better than Jay feared, with Ian skillfully deflecting Turner Senior's probing and shielding Jay from the spotlight.
Milestone: Hartwell celebration dinner at The Palladium (Friday, November 18)

What happens: A calmer Monday briefing lets Jay reframe Ian's old coldness as ordinary management rather than rejection. Kim circulates the holiday-event dates — the staff Christmas party and the black-tie end-of-year gala — and Jay weighs whether to visit family for Christmas.
Note: The exact party/gala dates Kim cites conflict with later chapters — see flags.

What happens: Ian buries himself in the Hartwell integration — sixteen-hour days, weekend work — specifically to smother his growing feelings for Jay. Jay quietly streamlines Ian's workload, and Ian's attempt at distraction only deepens his appreciation of him.

With the Hartwell crunch eased, the team decorates the office tree; paired with Ian on the garland, Jay rediscovers how much he enjoys Christmas. He decides to buy a small tree and decorations for his own apartment for the first time.

After work Jay orders a custom tuxedo for the gala, then buys and decorates a tiny tree at home and calls his mother to say he's staying in the city for Christmas. The next morning he shops for and ships gifts to his family.
Note: Jay tells the tailor the event is "January twenty fourth" — conflicts with other gala dates; see flags.

What happens: At the company Christmas party Jay feels fully part of the team; the couples' spa voucher stings, and Leo's pointed remark about Ian's lonely, family-less holidays leaves Jay quietly worried for him.
Milestone: Company Christmas party
Note: Ch7 gives this date as "December fifteenth" (a Thursday); the timeline follows the chapter's "Friday evening" — see flags.

What happens: The pre-holiday rush peaks; on the last day before the break a charged, lingering handshake passes between Jay and Ian. Jay spends Christmas content and alone with wine and his little tree, while Ian works through it alone in his spotless penthouse.
Milestone: Christmas (Sunday, December 25)

What happens: Ian wakes at 3 a.m. from a vivid dream of kissing Jay in his office and, rattled, summons Leo to freezing dawn tennis. He brutalizes him for three hours while Leo correctly names the fear underneath: Ian wants Jay and is terrified of it.
The office reopens to a skeleton crew between the holidays, and with Ela out sick it's just Jay, Luc, and Ian. Working late alone with Ian, Jay realizes how completely and unusually he trusts him.

Through the quiet week Jay shoulders more of Ian's work; on the year's last afternoon Ian shares his best whiskey with Jay and Luc, showing rare, easy warmth. Jay forgives Leo by text and spends New Year's Eve content and alone.
Milestone: Ian's New Year whiskey toast (Friday, December 30)

What happens: January reporting roars back to full throttle; at noon Jay's year-end bonus lands — $80,000, more money than he has ever held at once. Stunned, he treats himself to three tailored suits.
Milestone: $80,000 year-end bonus

What happens: Quarterly and annual reports grind on as gala buzz fills the office and Ela and Kim return. A restless Jay declines Luc's drinks, packs away his Christmas tree, and — curious about what Ian enjoys — finds his old solitude newly empty.
Jay collects his tuxedo and new suits, books a pricey last-minute haircut, and culls his old wardrobe — including the cheap interview suit he first arrived in — to ready himself for the gala.
Jay arrives at the Grand Hotel gala looking striking, and Ian — visibly affected and needled by Leo about the suitors circling — abandons his usual networking to hover protectively near his team.
Milestone: Annual black-tie gala
Note: Stated dates conflict (Ch7 "Jan 23," Ch8 "Jan 24") and the aftermath plays it as a Saturday; dated here Sat Jan 21 — see flags.

What happens: Separated from Ian and plied with drinks, Jay is cornered by a predatory client, Cruver, who gropes him and makes suggestive offers while the surrounding group stays oblivious. Shaken and ashamed, Jay flees to the restroom in tears.
Milestone: Cruver harassment incident

What happens: Ian finds Jay, slips them both out a back exit, and defuses the trauma with self-deprecating stories in the car. Over tea in Jay's modest apartment their connection deepens, though both stay carefully in denial.

Back at work the gala is harmless office gossip, and the team's own mortifying-party stories normalize Jay's night; Cruver's name surfaces as a known creep. Behind his door, Ian quietly begins building a case to make Cruver pay.

At his six-month review Ian offers Jay a promotion to analyst; torn between ambition and his wish to stay close to Ian, Jay takes the week to think and, on Monday, declines — choosing to remain Ian's assistant.
Milestone: Six-month review; Jay declines the analyst promotion

What happens: February opens with a heavy snowstorm that snarls Jay's commute; sharing the elevator with Ian, he becomes acutely, newly aware of his cologne. He brushes the reaction aside as the winter workload peaks.
Bitter cold defeats Jay's old coat, so on Saturday he buys a quality wool overcoat and scarf despite his frugal instincts. The gray weekend passes in solitary work.

Deep in "strategy season," Ian hands Jay a company credit card to use at his discretion, and Jay orders an elevated team dinner that lifts everyone's morale. Ian joins the team in the kitchen, deviating from his strict meal plan.
Milestone: Jay receives the company credit card

What happens: A stream of HR emails — the May retreat, the April quarterly party, vacation requests — keeps surfacing the retreat Jay isn't ready to contemplate. He decides to match his own time off to Ian's, once Ian submits any.

Working late alone together has become routine; Jay anticipates Ian's needs while Ian lingers to postpone his empty penthouse. Leo drops by and needles Ian about how much more relaxed Jay has made him.

A violent ice storm and power outage leave Jay drenched and forty-five minutes late for the first time in nearly seven months; in desperation he buys a full designer outfit from Hathaway's Menswear. He changes in the executive bathroom, unaware of how the new clothes affect Ian.
Colleagues react to Jay's transformed look with compliments that make him deeply self-conscious, while Ian finds concentration impossible. Both go home unsettled.

A scratchy throat on Wednesday becomes a serious flu; from Thursday Jay is bedridden and alone, diagnosed by telehealth and ordered off work for at least a week. The fever breaks Sunday, but his work phone dies and cuts him off.
Milestone: Jay's flu — first extended sick leave (from Thursday, February 23)

What happens: With Jay unreachable, Ian grows frantic — calling repeatedly, imagining the worst, realizing how necessary Jay has become. On Friday Jay revives his phone and texts that he'll return Monday, flooding Ian with relief.

Jay returns pale and thin; Ian presents a lavish "standard procedure" care package and insists he keep strict regular hours until fully recovered. The gesture warms Jay, but the illness has left him subdued and freshly aware of his isolation.
Milestone: Jay returns from sick leave; Ian's care package

What happens: Back at full strength in his second week, Jay rejoins the team lunches to find everyone's nerves frayed by winter and workload, bickering over where to eat. Ian's unchanging routine is the one steady thing.
Note: Jay's first week back (Mar 6–10) he ate at his desk, avoiding the cold.

What happens: A lawyer's call reveals Jay misfiled a legal notice and missed the Lagerstrom Holdings compliance deadline — due the day before, the Ides. Ian corrects him gently, but Jay spirals into shame and self-doubt.
Milestone: Jay's missed compliance deadline (Lagerstrom)

What happens: Seeing Jay crumbling, Luc walks him to a cozy café for spiked hot chocolate and shares his own disastrous rookie mistake. The reassurance loosens the knot of anxiety in Jay's chest.

As Jay finds his footing again, Ian quietly completes a three-month investigation into Orrey Capital Partners — Cruver's employer — and finalizes a memo recommending termination, to reach Turner Senior on Monday.
Note: The chapter calls the gala "three months ago"; from a January gala it is closer to two — see flags.

What happens: Word spreads that Turner & Associates has terminated Orrey amid a federal investigation; Ian confirms it at a Tuesday team meeting, and by Friday it is national news. Jay feels vindictive relief that he'll never face Cruver again.
Milestone: Orrey Capital Partners terminated (Cruver's employer under investigation)

What happens: April's first quarter-end brings a brutal reporting crunch — seven-day weeks, midnight finishes, a full Saturday in the office — that finally teaches Jay why the bonuses are so large. Ian submits the final file Tuesday at 6 p.m.
Milestone: Q1 quarterly reports submitted (Tuesday, April 11); quarterly bonus

What happens: As HR pushes "mentor" rooming for the May retreat, Leo engineers it so Ian and Jay will share a room. Ian protests furiously in private but, manipulated by his own protective instincts, gives in.
Milestone: Retreat rooming — Ian and Jay paired (Leo's scheme)

What happens: Jay stashes a spare suit at the office, joins a modest neighborhood gym, and starts training with Rus. His mother calls to ask him home for Ema's middle-school graduation at the end of June.
Note: Ema's end-of-June graduation is set up here for a later payoff.

What happens: At the spring quarterly party Jay is entirely at ease — eating well, joking with the team, neither snubbed nor singled out by Ian. Watching from across the room, Ian feels ashamed of the coldness he showed at Jay's first such party.
Note: Placement is tight against the Q1 crunch and pre-Easter cold snap — see flags.

What happens: A sudden cold snap sours the team's mood, so Jay uses the company card to bring everyone seasonal coffees — and, on impulse, orders Ian a sweet maple-bourbon latte unlike his usual black. Ian drinks the whole thing, touched by the unguarded thoughtfulness.
The cold snap drags on as the four-day Easter weekend approaches and the team trades holiday plans. Jay's intention to spend it on "industry reading" provokes affectionate horror from his colleagues.

Jay spends the Easter long weekend alone, working through advanced economics texts to quiet his anxiety about his modest credentials, pausing only for a grandmother-style holiday breakfast and his mother's call. He finishes one book and starts a harder one.
Milestone: Easter weekend (Jay studies alone)

What happens: The office returns refreshed to a sustainable pace; colleagues swap Easter stories and tease Jay for studying. He stays late again, unwilling to leave Ian working alone.

Spring settles in: Jay leads the team to Willow & Sage, sits in the park with strange ice cream, keeps up his Tuesday gym sessions with Rus, and reads management books to understand Ian's leadership. He feels a contentment he has never known.

After Jay leaves for the gym, Ian lingers at his desk and finds his annotated economics textbook; an hour of reading Jay's humble, curious margin notes — one puzzling over Ian's own foresight — makes Ian finally admit his feelings have outgrown his control.
Milestone: Ian reads Jay's margin notes — his attachment crystallizes (end of Arc 3)

What happens: Ian brings Jay along to a "relationship maintenance" visit with the Calder Wetherby Trust, driving through the city's old-money district that leaves Jay wide-eyed. The meeting is more whiskey and social ritual than business.
Milestone: Calder Wetherby Trust client visit

What happens: Over an expensive lunch Ian opens up about his privileged upbringing, creating unexpected intimacy. In the traffic-slowed drive back, Jay is acutely, uneasily aware of Ian's cologne and nearness.
Jay stuns the team by finally saying yes to Friday drinks; over a terrible craft beer he learns how much elaborate preparation the retreat — now two weeks off — will demand.
An exhausting fashion-district expedition for resort wear, swimwear, and a new suitcase costs Jay roughly two months of his old salary.
Realizing the sunset dinner is far dressier than he prepared for, Jay reluctantly returns to Hathaway's on Friday for an understated but expensive evening outfit.
The team buses to a beachfront resort, where Jay is assigned to share Seaview Suite 14 with Ian. At the afternoon art exhibition Ian buys the one abstract piece Jay genuinely liked.
Milestone: Company retreat begins

What happens: At the glamorous sunset dinner Jay gets properly drunk for the first time, oblivious to a colleague's persistent flirting. Rattled by the sight of him, Ian escorts him to their room and flees.
Hungover, Jay recovers under Ian's quiet care — water, painkillers, coffee — and at the pool Ian's swimming reveals a physique that sharpens Jay's awareness of his attraction.
Jay conquers his first scuba dive under Ian's and Zoe's patient guidance, the reef transformative. The beach bonfire dinner and a quiet fireside whiskey deepen the domesticity growing between them.

Ian's puzzle-solving wins the team coveted concert tickets in the treasure hunt; the retreat ends and an emboldened Jay decides to attend Ema's graduation. At home, Ian clears a wall for the painting Jay admired.
Milestone: Company retreat ends

What happens: The office buzzes with retreat photos and diving footage; Ian approves Jay's day off for Ema's June graduation and offers a company car for the trip.
Concert-ticket fever takes over the office; Ian gives his pair to Kim and Ela, then is roped by Max into attending the show with the whole team.
The team descends on the underground club Substrate, where Leo turns up — having planted the prize himself — and the punishing experimental set bewilders Jay.
Milestone: Underground concert at Substrate

What happens: Jay and Ian retreat to the bar and bond over their shared philistine bafflement; Ian reveals he played violin seriously through college, then drives Jay home.

A brutal heat wave drives Jay to the air-conditioned office every evening alongside Ian; he keeps up his gym sessions, takes driving lessons for the trip home, and starts browsing apartment listings. Real-world note: corroborated — June 2017 brought a record Western-European heatwave (upper 30s °C in Germany, peaking 23–24 June). Play it as a real, record-breaking summer. See `timeline/real-world-reference.md`.
In the languid summer lull Jay reorganizes the filing system and broadens his grasp of how the firm operates, while the heat reshapes the lunch debate around air-conditioning quality.

Jay drives the company "limousine" home for Ema's Friday graduation; the luxury car and designer clothes make him a sensation and he completely overshadows her, staying an extra day to ferry her friends around.
Milestone: Ema's graduation (Jay visits family)
Note: Ch18 names the date "June twenty-fourth (a Friday)," but June 24, 2017 is a Saturday; the ceremony reads as Friday, so dated Fri Jun 23 — see flags.

What happens: Jay sets a family photo on his desk; when his own attempts to photograph the office for Ema fail comically, Ian steps in to take proper shots — including a candid selfie Jay quietly treasures.
Milestone: Ian and Jay's office selfie

What happens: July opens with Q2 quarterly reporting and a second quarterly bonus; as colleagues scatter on summer leave, Jay refuses to file his own vacation unless Ian finally takes time off too.
Milestone: Q2 quarterly reports; second quarterly bonus

What happens: At the outdoor summer quarterly party Jay and Ian share a quiet fountain-side moment under the torchlight; Luc warns him, pointedly, about the perils of office romance.
Milestone: Summer quarterly party

What happens: Jay turns thirty and reaches his goal of being completely debt-free, celebrating with a punishing birthday workout from Rus and his first-ever social-media post. Across the city, Ian agonizes over — and never sends — a birthday text.
Milestone: Jay's 30th birthday; becomes debt-free
Note: The text says July 14 "fell on a Saturday"; in 2017 it is a Friday — see flags.

What happens: Newly thirty, Jay reflects on how completely his year has transformed him and handles an urgent Wentworth consultation with ease, feeling he genuinely belongs.
Ian notices Jay's standing Tuesday gym departure, and their unhurried evening conversations edge toward real friendship; Jay shrugs off Rus's nudges toward a more balanced life.
Kim and Ela return from their not-so-secret shared vacation; Luc confides his fraying relationship, and Jay recognizes that his own reason for staying has become belonging, not ambition.

At his one-year review Ian re-offers the analyst role — Jay again declines — and gives him an extravagant watch, part anniversary gift, part apology for his first-year failures. That night Ian finally admits to himself that he is hopelessly in love with Jay.
Milestone: One-year anniversary at Turner & Associates; Ian's watch gift — end of Book 1

What happens: The morning after privately admitting he is hopelessly in love (Book 1's close), Ian skips lunch and broods; Jay notices, brings him soup and then ginger-mint lemonade, openly fussing over Ian's health for the first time. The boss/assistant dynamic has quietly inverted.
Note: Picks up immediately from Book 1 Ch20.

What happens: Ian resolves to manage his feelings by ensuring Jay's flourishing. By week's end he schedules a week of leave for the **last week of October**; Jay coordinates the same dates and books a tropical beginner-diving vacation (Lagoon Belt). Zoe returns from her own diving trip and offers to help Jay prepare.
Milestone: Ian takes vacation for the first time; Jay books his solo diving trip

What happens: Jay's first local diving lesson with instructor Ina, Zoe along for support; dinner afterward turns into a real conversation about apartment-hunting and city life. Ian sits alone in the office, recognizing his feeling as something close to jealousy.
Milestone: Jay and Zoe's friendship begins

What happens: A small, warm morning exchange about the diving lesson sustains Ian all day; he stalls at his desk to keep Jay near and is needled by an irrational jealousy when Zoe shares her number with Jay. August heat lies over the financial district.

Jay and Zoe spend a brutally hot Saturday touring neighbourhoods for Jay's apartment search; over dinner Zoe comes out as gay (she too chose Turner & Associates for Ian's anti-romance reputation), and Jay, prompted, recognizes himself as "ace." A genuine friendship forms.
Milestone: Zoe comes out; Jay first considers himself asexual

What happens: With Max and Luc now on holiday, Jay spirals over the October trip (he has never flown or left the country). Mid-week Ian reassures him; on **Friday (Aug 25)** Ian announces a business trip to the **New Continent (Garnet Ridge Group) for the end of September** and invites Jay as a "training opportunity" — really to ease Jay's travel fears.
Milestone: New Continent business trip arranged
Note: Overlaps Ch21's final days — see flags.

What happens: Leo turns up at Ian's penthouse with scotch and teases him mercilessly about engineering "alone time" with Jay, insisting Jay is "head over heels" in love. After Leo leaves, Ian breaks months of control and, for the first time, lets himself fantasize.

Leo corners Jay to arrange "international face" grooming — celebrity stylist and tailor appointments on the company account — and, more seriously, warns Jay to be gentle with Ian's fragile, guarded heart. The seed of "what if Ian feels something" is planted.

Jay's first fitting at **Saviour & Sons**, the city's most patronizing tailor (Leo's choice); the staff treat him as a "project" to be made attractive, and the humiliation reopens his class wound. He retreats to Café Lemon and Fay's warmth to recover.
Milestone: First tailor fitting (the "three-piece suits" commissioned)

What happens: Max and Luc return to full staffing; the late-summer heat breaks. Jay reclaims a normal life — Tuesday gym, café visits, swimming, his **second diving lesson (Fri Sep 1)** — and endures the **second tailor fitting (Sat Sep 2)**. Ian quietly mourns losing their late evenings together.
Note: The "end of August" label runs a few days into early September; the tailor-fitting spacing is inconsistent across chapters — see flags.

What happens: "When September started," the weather turns lovely but Jay, stung by the tailor's contempt, withdraws — leaving at five for parks, gym, café, swimming, and his **third diving lesson (Fri Sep 8)**. Ian notices the new chill and can't place its cause.

Luc takes sudden week-long sick leave; Jay covers his work, staying late with Ian again under a strange new tension. On Friday Jay finally asks Ian whether he's done something wrong; both deflect, each privately fearing the worst.

Saturday brings the gym and the **final tailor fitting**; Sunday the **fourth diving lesson**. Luc returns Monday looking gutted. Jay postpones his apartment search to November as a week of "performed civility" with Ian grinds on.
Milestone: Tailoring complete

What happens: Luc texts late and Jay goes to his (absurdly lavish, four-roommate) flat with whiskey; Luc reveals his eight-year partner Cam dumped him. They trade their worst breakups (Jay's college humiliation), and Jay stays the night — his first-ever sleepover at thirty.
Milestone: Luc's breakup; Jay and Luc's friendship deepens

What happens: Luc improves; the team showers Jay with travel advice. Jay collects his three-piece suits **(Wed Sep 27)**, the celebrity haircut **(Thu Sep 28 a.m.)**, posts a "First business trip" selfie for Ema, and is ready for the afternoon departure.

A limousine collects Jay at noon. In the car Ian explains he is "a pariah in high society" — banned from Saviour & Sons himself since his scandal — recasting Jay's humiliation. Ian promises a Saturday shopping trip for Jay's siblings.
Milestone: New Continent business trip begins (Jay's first flight)
Note: Departs end of September, "before the chaos of quarter-end in October."

What happens: Jay's first flight — an eight-hour business-class trip — goes smoothly; he blurts that "having you here makes everything better." They arrive at the five-star hotel (adjacent rooms) at 5 p.m. local time; Ian force-marches Jay through dinner to beat the jet lag.

A full day with CEO Mac Cartwright. At the boisterous client dinner a drunk executive, **Zac**, calls Jay a "beautiful escort"; Ian's fury is instant and he terminates the deal on the spot. Unable to sleep, the two share a quiet midnight whiskey at the hotel bar.
Milestone: The "escort" insult; Ian kills the deal

What happens: Mac fires Zac, apologizes publicly, and salvages the partnership with a tech-lab tour and a prototype laptop gifted to Jay. Ian keeps his promise: an afternoon shopping spree (company card) for sibling gifts, then dinner atop the city's tallest building.
Milestone: Deal saved; sibling shopping spree

What happens: A six-hour weather delay strands them in the airport, where an exhausted, defenceless Jay confesses his impostor feelings and Ian counters that "harassment is the harasser's fault." They land home around 3 a.m. **Monday, October 2**; both take the day to recover.
Milestone: Return from New Continent

What happens: Jay returns to a studio overflowing with luggage and ten suits. At the office he and Ian process the trip; Jay resolves he wants to be Ian's "anchor" — someone dependable for a man the world treated too harshly.
Note: The narration's "with the month drawing to a close" reads as late September, but the firm anchors place this in early October — see flags.

What happens: Jay distributes souvenirs (and comfort chocolate for Luc), notably more confident and outgoing — even proposing a future "something fun." He gives gifts to Rus and Fay on his way home.

Q3 quarterly reports hit "like a plague"; the quarterly bonus lands almost unnoticed. Ten brutal days (including the weekend) keep Jay and Ian constantly surrounded by the team, smothering the private time Ian had hoped for. Reports go out **Thursday (Oct 12)**; the spent team drifts through the **autumn quarterly party (Fri Oct 13)**.
Milestone: Q3 reports; quarterly bonus; autumn quarterly party

What happens: Jay patches things up with his family by phone (and gifts his old laptop to Ari), rebuilds his gym routine with Rus, and **finishes the diving course** with Ina. Evening coffee conversations with Ian resume at a sustainable pace.

After warm farewells (and a charged near-goodbye with Ian), Jay flies solo to Lagoon Belt. He earns his **open-water certification**, proves solo travel is not just survivable but enjoyable, and returns **Saturday, November 4** to chilly autumn air — Halloween having passed entirely while he was away.
Milestone: Solo diving vacation; open-water certification
Note: "Last week of October" is approximate — the trip spans **Halloween (Tue Oct 31)** and ends in early November; see flags.

What happens: Tanned and confident, Jay returns to find his postcard ("wish you were here") pinned to Ian's calendar. Ian — having spent his own week alone, walking his old university town and finally accepting he is in love — resolves to simply love Jay "from the shadows."
Milestone: Jay back from vacation; Ian privately commits to the status quo

What happens: A long extended evening conversation about the trip and Jay's housing plans. Tuesday's gym session reveals Fay is out with early flu; Jay reminds himself to book a flu shot and calls his family, warm and content.

Luc calls in Jay's promised "something fun." Ian deadpans "fencing"; Jay, against all sense, **books a group fencing session for the following Friday** and ropes in Rus and his gym friends. Thursday he finally schedules apartment viewings.

The fencing night turns into Luc's flatmates vs. Rus's crew; Jay loses every match but his team (anchored by ex-military Van) wins. Drinks and war stories follow; Jay realizes he's made real outside-of-work friends.
Milestone: Fencing night; Jay's friendship with Rus's circle

What happens: A week of apartment viewings (over twenty units) leaves Jay with a meticulous folder but no clear winner. On Friday Luc guilt-trips him into a **blind date "tomorrow"**; Jay reluctantly agrees, half-planning to call Ian for rescue if it goes badly.

At the gym even Rus pushes Jay toward the date and warns him off his boss — which only makes Jay defiant. The date is a disaster: a preening guest, **Yan**, mocks Jay as a "funeral director" and "accidentally" spills red wine on his best suit. Jay flees and texts Ian: "The evening did go badly."
Milestone: The blind date

What happens: Ian arrives within minutes, rushes the jacket to an emergency cleaner, and takes Jay for a drink. Jay drinks far more than usual ("being drunk is only dangerous when you're around people who'd take advantage — which you're not"); Ian half-carries him home and, worried, stays the night on the couch.

Jay wakes mortified — not at impropriety but at having made his "well-bred" boss sleep on a shabby couch. Over breakfast Ian reveals his own past: disowned with nothing, he **sold his watch and lived eight years in a tiny studio** before rebuilding. The gap between their worlds narrows.
Milestone: Ian's disownment/poverty backstory revealed

What happens: Each shares their deepest wound — Ian's self-blame for the Sam Connelly scandal, Jay's harassment at his first job and his mother's protective response. Jay hugs Ian, telling him "it was not your fault." Both feel the weight of unprecedented trust.

Over lunch Jay invites himself into Ian's weekend to force him to rest: he'll help with spreadsheets **next Saturday (Dec 2)** so Ian must take **Sunday (Dec 3)** off, and Jay will plan an outing. Both are quietly thrilled.
Milestone: The "weekend together" is set

What happens: A new closeness colours the office. Luc reports the blind-date aftermath (Gav mortified, Yan disgraced). Jay calls Ian's recommended real-estate agent and books a Friday viewing.

Jay realizes he's keeping his weekend plans with Ian secret — and finds the secrecy thrilling rather than alarming. He plans a personal itinerary (ferry, pumpkin bistro, botanical garden). Friday's agent shows ten overpriced units.

Jay spends the day at Ian's penthouse doing spreadsheet work — first time truly in Ian's space (the retreat painting on the wall, May the housekeeper's fruit platters). Ian cooks; they talk about food, housing, and (briefly) Sam Connelly. Dinner out caps the day.
Milestone: Jay's first day inside Ian's home

What happens: Jay's "not-a-date": a scenic ferry ride, a rustic pumpkin-themed bistro, a botanical garden, caramel apples, and **feeding ducks**. Ian admits that during his studio years he "never allowed himself" any leisure. It is the most equal, domestic day they've shared — and afterward Jay is hollowed by melancholy.
Milestone: The autumn "date"

What happens: Back behind the glass wall, both ache for what the weekend revealed and can't name it. They blame the dim late-autumn weather; Jay skips dinners and grows worried he's catching something.
Note: The prose's "as November slowly ended" lags the calendar (this is early December) — see flags.

What happens: Ian's agent shows Jay a flawless, fully-furnished riverside flat — balcony, river view, twelve minutes' walk from Ian's building — for a suspiciously low 1.5 million. Jay is charmed and deeply distrustful, certain it's "another elaborate plot."
Milestone: Jay finds (and fears) the riverside apartment

What happens: Jay asks Ian about the agent's reliability; Ian (who secretly bribed the agent to settle Jay near him) deflects. The team mostly cheers the deal; only Max and Kim are cautious. Jay senses an "undercurrent of manipulation" he can't decode.

Rus (echoing Ian) urges Jay to seize the apartment. Their "fun activity" is **outdoor laser tag** in cold drizzle — wet, filthy, miserable. Jay declines an Ian rescue but a check-in text lifts his mood.
Milestone: Laser tag (the second disastrous "fun" outing)

What happens: Jay splurges on a spa day, then canvasses everyone — mother, Fay, more viewings — about the apartment; all encourage him. He gets his **flu vaccination (~Wed Dec 13)**. "November had ended" and the gloom deepens his fear of a major decision.

The office decorating splits into two trees, separating Jay and Ian — so Ian orders a small tree for his office and they decorate it alone (gold and silver, "for his hair" / "for his eyes"). Jay decides to **delay the apartment choice until after Christmas** and to spend the holiday with Ian rather than face his step-grandparents.
Milestone: Office Christmas decorating; Jay chooses to spend Christmas with Ian
Note: Tree-decorating placement vs. the Christmas party is approximate — see flags.

What happens: The **company Christmas party** (jewellery-voucher gifts this year). Jay then spends the weekend gift-shopping for his family, choosing his own first cologne and an engraved lighter ("You've got this") for Ian.
Milestone: Company Christmas party
Note: Dated as the last pre-holiday Friday; gift-shipping timing is tight — see flags.

What happens: Jay spends Christmas at Ian's: they decorate a tree, cook a feast in matching aprons, and exchange gifts (the lighter; sapphire **cufflinks and tiepin** for Jay). A perfect, domestic, mutually adoring day — neither crosses the line.
Milestone: Christmas Day together; Ian's cufflinks/tiepin gift

What happens: The skeleton-crew holiday week (Jay, Luc, Zoe, Ian). Jay caters daily bakery lunches; he and Ian drift to the lounge each evening, the glass wall "dissolved." Luc's promotion talk visibly tenses Ian.

Work ends at 3 p.m. and Ian repeats last year's **whiskey toast** — now "a tradition." A long handshake with Jay. At midnight Jay texts "thank you for making this year extraordinary," and Ian answers at once.
Milestone: New Year's Eve whiskey toast (Dec 31)

What happens: **Annual reporting** roars back; Jay's **year-end bonus is more than double his entire previous year's income**. After a brutal two weeks, the **annual black-tie gala (Sat Jan 20, 2018)** — Ian, rattled by a nightmare of Jay dying, must fly out Thursday for a client emergency and cancels their planned day out. They drink at Jay's old studio; Ian slips out "You looked beautiful tonight." Jay decides to **buy the apartment**; he meets the (improbably generous) mortgage bank Ian arranged, and the month ends with strategy season looming.
Milestone: Year-end bonus; annual gala; Jay commits to the apartment
Note: Gala dated Sat Jan 20, matching the real calendar (cf. Book 1's January gala).

What happens: A wet, snowless winter. A citywide metro flood makes Jay an hour late to re-view the riverside flat; the swollen river and dark parks feel ominous and he takes the contract home unsigned. He drifts to Ian's neighbourhood; they share bitter tea.

Deep in strategy season, Jay accidentally **drops a database table** (the analytical scoring history, no backup) — the exact error he was trained to avoid. Ian calmly summons emergency IT; Jay goes home and cries himself to sleep.
Milestone: Jay's dropped-table error

What happens: Jay numbly packs his studio over the weekend (uncovering his **10,000-cash emergency envelope**), doubting both the move and the job. **Monday (Feb 12)** IT restores the table — but the relief doesn't dent his conviction that he's fundamentally deficient.

Jay realizes he **threw out the cash envelope** with the trash (collected Monday — gone). The personal failure crushes him; he **cancels the apartment purchase** and fantasizes about retreating to a low-stakes life. Friday Ian gently checks in; Jay shuts him out.
Milestone: Jay loses his emergency savings; cancels the apartment

What happens: "The next two weeks were filled with self-pity." Rus, Luc, and Ian all try and fail to reach Jay. Ian — buried in the fourteen-hour days his own decisions created — watches helplessly, the lighter's "You've got this" mocking him, as Jay "slowly disappears."
Jay polishes his CV and blasts fifty-plus firms; a week of silence batters his ego further. "As March started," a consulting firm finally offers an interview.
Milestone: Jay begins job-hunting

What happens: HR's third email announces the **company retreat — a lakeside mountain resort, end of May, singles rooms**. The team buzzes over activities; Jay reads it knowing he likely won't be there. He requests Thursday off (for the interview) without explanation.
Milestone: May retreat announced (single rooms this year)

What happens: Jay interviews at a mid-sized firm (half his salary) and is startlingly relaxed — months at the top have seasoned him. He lies smoothly about wanting "career development," then goes home and weeps at the thought of never seeing Ian again.

Jay realizes the pain of leaving Ian dwarfs any work stress. Over endless coffee at Café Lemon, Fay names it: "You're head over heels in love." Meanwhile Ian correctly deduces Jay interviewed elsewhere and starts drafting a retention plan.
Milestone: Fay names Jay's love; Ian resolves to keep him

What happens: A "men's night out" with Rus, Mic, and Van; all three diagnose Jay as in love. A drunken sing-along earns a noise complaint. Hungover and alone (no Ian to tend him), Jay concludes he can't leave — he'll **stay and endure**, however much it hurts.
Milestone: Jay decides to stay

What happens: The office is a disaster zone — colleagues literally living in sleeping bags. Jay's resolve wavers but holds; he gets a **second-interview call**. Seeing Ian on the verge of tears through the glass shakes him.
Jay attends the relaxed second interview (Accord Financial). He leaves more certain than ever that he'd trade the skyscrapers and prestige for an ordinary crowd — but **not** for losing Ian.

At **2:15 p.m. the strategy is submitted** and the team collapses in relief. A job offer arrives by phone — and Jay **declines it**, choosing to stay. Ian, misreading the smiling call as an acceptance, flees to the bathroom in despair, then resolves to win Jay back Monday.
Milestone: Strategy season ends; Jay declines the outside offer

What happens: At his delayed review Ian apologizes for the punishing season and offers a **new contract: permanent, ~45% raise, "coordinator" title, executive benefits**. Jay, suspicious it's all to keep him, accepts — and decides he'll **buy the apartment after all**.
Milestone: Jay's new contract and title; reverses the apartment cancellation

What happens: Jay re-engages the agent and bank. Ian announces the team will **expand — interviews in April, hires by May for the retreat**. Jay learns Ian is "notoriously picky," and HR offers keep arriving (Jay politely declines them all).
Milestone: Hiring expansion announced

What happens: The crisis past, daily lunches resume (Willow & Sage soup at last). Jay signs up for retreat activities — **hike, cooking class, paddle boats** — and arranges to share a boat with Ian, blushing as Fay's "head over heels" verdict echoes.

Jay submits the mortgage paperwork (two weeks to completion) and, newly settled, calls his mother to arrange an **Easter visit home**. He shops for groceries, sibling gifts, and — easily now — casual clothes.
Milestone: Mortgage application submitted; Easter visit planned

What happens: **Q1 quarterly reporting** begins, but the streamlined Hartwell processes make it far more bearable. Jay's salary lands at **80,000 (four times normal)** — back-pay, quarterly bonus, and strategy bonus combined. Reports go out the following Monday.
Milestone: Q1 reports; the 80,000 paycheck

What happens: Overtime ends; talk turns to Easter and the party. Jay secures the **company limousine** and, at the **spring quarterly party (Thu Apr 12)**, watches Luc flirt and reflects — with deliberate denial — on how magnetic Ian is.
Milestone: Spring quarterly party

What happens: The Easter long weekend at his mother's: cooking, painting eggs, ferrying his siblings in the limousine, gift-giving. His mother, tearful, says she's proud; Jay reflects warmly on his childhood despite everything.
Milestone: Easter at home (Jay's first family visit since Ema's graduation)
Note: The manuscript places Easter in mid-April, *after* Q1 reports; the real 2018 Easter was **April 1** — see flags.

What happens: Back from Easter, Jay learns his **mortgage paperwork is ready**; he signs Wednesday and gets the keys, with the move set for Saturday. Rus's crew and several coworkers — and, pointedly, Ian — sign on to help.

Jay splurges joyfully on the new home (towels, bath products, a new plant pot, dishes). Overwhelmed by packages, he calls Ian, who comes over and helps him haul and unpack until eleven; they tour the huge storage unit Ian clearly arranged.

Moving day: Rus, Mic, and Van with a van; Zoe and Luc; Ian (in rare casual wear) with a painting; and an uninvited **Leo** bearing housewarming gifts (and making amends). Two worlds mix easily; the move takes under three hours.
Milestone: Jay moves into the riverside apartment

What happens: An impromptu, rowdy housewarming (Leo's sound system, Van's shirtless fitness demo, Rus bluntly telling Ian his management is poor — which Ian concedes). After everyone leaves, Ian cleans up and, at Jay's wistful request, **stays the night on the couch**.

A hungover, blissful first morning — coffee, the cold balcony, and Jay handing Ian a **spare key** "in case of emergency." The new ten-minute commute begins; that week Ian is away each afternoon **interviewing senior-analyst candidates**.
Milestone: Jay gives Ian a spare key

What happens: **Ray Shore** — Ian's brilliant, blunt, socially indifferent former classmate — joins as senior analyst. He grills Ian without deference, claims the far desk, and leaves at 4 p.m. sharp. Ian is visibly at ease with him.
Milestone: Ray Shore hired (first new senior analyst)

What happens: Ray audits the whole methodology; the team adapts to his refusal of overtime and pleasantries. Ian asks **Jay to co-conduct next week's junior-analyst interviews**, and to let new hires take over Jay's administrative load — unsettling Jay, who fears losing his usefulness.

Four candidates each afternoon. Ian, wary of charm, defers to Jay's read on character; they settle on **Jay's top two** (a modest woman struggling with industry culture, and an awkward but humane man). Ian's trust thrills and aches Jay in equal measure.
Milestone: Junior analysts selected (Jay's picks)

What happens: Jay terminates his old lease (meeting set), photographs the new flat for his family, and can't stop intrusive thoughts of being held by Ian — fantasies he scolds himself for entertaining.

Both junior hires accept (starting in June, with a second senior analyst, **Liv Moreau**). Ray refuses the retreat outright. We learn Ian engineered Ray's hire (via Leo) specifically to lighten the workload — and reclaim his evenings with Jay. The retreat is two days off.

Ian collects Jay at 7 a.m. (emergency key, underground garage); the team departs at 4 p.m. on luxury coaches for the six-hour drive to **Pinewood Resort**. Jay sits beside Ian, they talk the whole way, and arrive at 10 p.m. in rising thunder — adjacent rooms.
Milestone: Company retreat begins (storm-bound)

What happens: Storms cancel outdoor activities. Jay and Ian attend a **scent workshop** and create a **shared fragrance** — Ian's warmth and Jay's freshness blended — then a meditative tea ceremony. A small, charged intimacy.
Milestone: Jay and Ian's shared scent

What happens: A fashion show, photo shoot, and old-cinema dinner under a glass roof split by lightning. Stylists put Ian in an avant-garde, skin-tight outfit (Leo's doing) that he wears with disarming confidence; Jay can't stop staring. They escape early and talk by the fire until 1 a.m.

A cook-off: Ian's masterful kitchen skills win the finance team decisively (prize TBA). After concerts (Ian rapt at a string quartet), they retreat to Ian's room and drink far too much **absinthe**, trading their deepest fears, until Jay drifts off in Ian's bed.
Milestone: Finance wins the cook-off; the absinthe night

What happens: Both wake brutally hungover in Ian's bed (nothing happened). They recover side by side at the spa. Leo reveals the cook-off prize: an **all-expenses thermal-springs weekend for the finance team** (Ian smells a trap). They drive home, arriving at 8 p.m.
Milestone: The thermal-springs prize awarded (the Book 3 hot-springs trip is set in motion)

What happens: Onboarding day: junior analysts **Rin Harris** and **Gal Gray**, plus senior analyst **Liv Moreau** — warm, charming, and (to Ian) immediately suspect precisely because of it. Ray remains aloof.
Milestone: Liv, Rin, and Gal join the team

What happens: Jay returns the keys to his old studio (full deposit back) and, alone in the empty room, grieves the refuge it had been — crying that night in the new apartment despite himself.
Milestone: Old lease ended

What happens: Liv charms the whole team; Jay stays guarded, trusting Ian's wariness. An **integration party at Murphy's (Fri)** — Ian attends, then leaves early, consumed by jealousy watching Jay surrounded by charming women, convinced he is slowly losing him.

**Gal's attention turns personal and excessive** — uninvited coffee, manufactured questions, requests to be shown around Jay's neighbourhood. Ian, recognizing the pattern of his own old betrayal, grows alarmed. Rin, by contrast, befriends Luc and tries (in vain) to crack Ray.
Characters: Ian, Jay, Luc, Gal, Rin, Ray

What happens: As **July quarterly reports** loom, the senior analysts train up. Ray bluntly shuts down Gal's hovering. Ian begins **driving Jay home each night** — a small, savoured intimacy he's longed to allow himself.
Milestone: Ian starts driving Jay home

What happens: **Q2 quarterly reports** descend. Gal keeps disrupting Jay's work despite warnings; Ian formally redirects her. The evening drives home continue, the only peace in the chaos.

Reports submitted Wednesday. Jay requests the following **Friday off** for his family's visit; Ian asks to come over for **Jay's birthday (this Sunday)** with a gift. In the kitchen, Jay's kindness to a sulking Gal lands wrong — she fixates on him.

At the **summer quarterly party**, a drunk Gal corners Jay; Ian extracts him to the terrace. Ian names Gal's conduct as harassment and vows to handle it. Standing close in the city lights, **Jay finally admits to himself he is in love with Ian** — deeply, hopelessly. He goes home and weeps into Ian's burgundy blanket.
Milestone: Summer quarterly party; Jay admits his love (to himself)

What happens: After a Saturday gym session, **Jay's 31st birthday (Sunday)**: Ian visits with a thoughtful **coffee maker** and blend selection, and they spend the afternoon on the balcony. Ian offers to run with Jay sometime. The gift says "I pay attention. I notice what you need" — gift and agony at once.
Milestone: Jay's 31st birthday
Note: Jay's canonical birthday is **July 14**; the manuscript narrates it as a Sunday (July 14, 2018 is a Saturday) — see flags.

What happens: Ian formally warns Gal (she cries, then turns passive-aggressive). Rus and Luc conspire to throw Jay a birthday party; Jay relents. Leo's true aim surfaces: a "test run" to **soften Ian toward his own upcoming 40th-birthday celebration (in September)**. Ian quietly decides to fire Gal at month's end. Jay's family departs Thursday at 2 p.m.
Milestone: Leo plants the seed of Ian's 40th-birthday party (the Book 3 opener)

What happens: Jay's mother and siblings arrive for the weekend; he cooks a homey welcome dinner and gives up his bed for a floor mattress. Friday is a full city tour — the office lobby (where the receptionist knows his name), ice cream, Willow & Sage, his old neighbourhood, Café Lemon.
Milestone: Jay's family visits the new apartment
Note: The manuscript calls Jay's day off "Friday the nineteenth," but July 19, 2018 is a Thursday — see flags.

What happens: Birthday-shopping with the family by day; that evening **Ian arrives to escort Jay** to the party and gives a second gift — **onyx-and-white-gold cufflinks and tiepin** — fastening them himself, an intimacy that leaves Jay breathless. Leo has hijacked the styling and venue.

The party is at **The Palladium** (Leo's extravagance). Jay's coworkers, gym friends, and family mingle warmly; his mother is pointedly cool toward Ian ("rich men don't do things without reasons"). Jay is happy and surrounded by people who care. Leo even books a stylist appointment as a "gift."
Milestone: Jay's birthday party at The Palladium

What happens: Gal corners Jay in the archive room; he firmly tells her to leave — then she **files a false assault complaint**. Surveillance footage instantly exonerates Jay; **Gal is fired on the spot** and referred to legal. Leo comes over with takeout and reveals Ian once weathered an identical false accusation years ago — which is why the building has cameras everywhere now.
Milestone: Gal's false complaint; her termination

What happens: Jay reels with guilt for having recommended Gal but resolves anew to **stay by Ian's side, whatever it costs**. Ian, blaming himself for delaying her dismissal, comes to Jay's apartment uninvited and finally **pulls him into a long embrace**. Jay asks if they can still take their cancelled winter outing "someday"; Ian says yes. Both, separated by a wall, ache for the other — and both choose to keep loving in silence. **End of Book 2.**
Milestone: The embrace; both deeper in love, still unconfessed — end of Book 2

What happens: Two years to the day since Jay arrived (and the morning after Ian's embrace closed Book 2). With Gal gone, the office breathes again. Over an after-work dinner — pointedly *not* a business dinner — Ian thanks Jay for staying and calls them "companions." Jay goes home and admits, fully, that he is completely in love.
Milestone: Jay's two-year anniversary; the first "companions" dinner
Note: Picks up directly from Book 2 Ch40 (the embrace was ~Wed Aug 1).
Keywords: two-year anniversary, "companions", not-a-business-dinner, confession-to-self
Purpose: Sets the year's emotional baseline — the "companions" frame both will spend the book failing to name — and confirms Jay is fully, privately in love.
Chapter theme: Settling into the post-Gal calm; rituals that braid their lives together.
Arc theme: Building a shared world — new traditions, the fortieth-birthday gala, and a hot-springs retreat where closeness first tips past the safe line.
Book theme: Summer into winter — wanting; desire surfaces and goes unclaimed, and the fake-couple pretence dissolves the line between game and truth.

What happens: A gym session with Rus (Jay finally tells him the full Gal story), then the dreaded tailor fitting (a birthday gift from Leo). That afternoon Leo barges in with champagne and a scheme: a magnificent fortieth-birthday party for Ian. Leo names what's obvious — how much Ian matters to Jay — and recruits him to "test the waters."
Milestone: Leo proposes Ian's 40th-birthday party
Keywords: Rus and the Gal story, tailor fitting, Leo's champagne scheme, "test the waters"
Purpose: Outside eyes (Leo) name Jay's feeling before Jay will, and fire the starting gun on the gala that drives the whole arc.

What happens: Jay sounds Ian out; Ian reluctantly agrees to the party ("no party hats, no speeches"). Stuck on what to give a man who has everything, Jay decides on **homemade infusions/tinctures** in his grandfather's style, and turns his kitchen into a small distillery. **Tia Larson** is settled on as Gal's replacement. Slow, well-staffed summer (Ray and Liv keep overtime away).
Characters: Ian, Jay, Liv, Gal, Ray, Tia
Milestone: Ian agrees to the party; Jay begins the tincture gift; Tia chosen
Keywords: homemade tinctures, grandfather's craft, Tia chosen, "no party hats, no speeches"
Purpose: Turns the class gap into a love language — Jay's answer to "what do you give a man who has everything" is the one thing only he can make.

What happens: Jay reasons himself into "safe love" — he'll want nothing, demand nothing, just keep showing up — even as intrusive thoughts about being touched by Ian intrude. Over a lounge coffee, Ian gently corrects Jay's memory of his early days: he was never a "drama" like Gal, just frightened and trying to disappear, which "broke my heart."
Keywords: "safe love", intrusive thoughts of being touched, "broke my heart", the frightened early days
Purpose: Names Jay's coping doctrine — want nothing, demand nothing, just keep showing up — the denial engine of the book, while Ian rewrites Jay's shame about who he was at the start.

What happens: A new tradition: **Tuesday evening runs** along the river with Ian (replacing Jay's gym slot). Jay struggles; Ian coaches patiently. Swimming, exercise, and another **Friday dinner** thread their lives tighter together.
Milestone: The Tuesday-run tradition begins
Weather: warm late-summer evenings by the river
Keywords: Tuesday runs begin, patient coaching, swimming, Friday dinner
Purpose: Establishes the Tuesday-run ritual whose loss and revival will track their closeness across the rest of the book.

What happens: Jay arranges to co-fund a car for Tim's eighteenth. Max and Zoe return from holiday. On **Wednesday (~Aug 22)** Leo unveils embossed invitations to a ballroom gala — "about a hundred people" (a lie) — "in four and a half weeks." Ian, over Friday dinner, again loses the argument that Jay should take vacation alone.
Characters: Ian, Jay, Leo, Zoe, Max, Tim
Milestone: Gala invitations issued ("4½ weeks out")
Keywords: co-funding Tim's car, embossed invitations, "about a hundred people" (a lie), vacation argument
Purpose: Sets the gala's runaway scale and re-states the wealth gulf, while Ian quietly wins the right to share Jay's vacation.
Chapter theme: The gala's gravity pulls everyone in; old ghosts and small jealousies surface.
Arc theme: Building a shared world — new traditions, the fortieth-birthday gala, and a hot-springs retreat where closeness first tips past the safe line.
Book theme: Summer into winter — wanting; desire surfaces and goes unclaimed, and the fake-couple pretence dissolves the line between game and truth.

What happens: Rus reveals he, Mic, and Van were invited (Leo over-invited wildly). That evening Ian takes Jay to a **classical concert** — which sours when memories of Sam Connelly (who feigned a love of classical music) intrude; Ian goes distant, and each blames himself. Monday, **Ema calls**: Leo invited her too, and she desperately wants to attend.
Milestone: The concert (Sam Connelly's ghost); Ema's invitation surfaces
Keywords: the concert, Sam Connelly's ghost, Ian goes distant, Ema's invitation surfaces
Purpose: Surfaces Ian's defining wound to show how it still sabotages tenderness — the haunted concert the island will later be written over.

What happens: Leo is unreachable for two weeks; Ema's pleading texts pile up; Jay's mother refuses. **The final tailor fitting (Sat ~Sep 1)** completes Jay's suit collection. Sunday Leo finally answers — he was abroad, hunting Ian's gift across **three countries** — which sends Jay spiraling that his homemade tinctures are inadequate.
Milestone: Tailoring complete; Leo's globe-trotting gift hunt revealed
Keywords: tailoring complete, Leo's three-country gift hunt, tincture doubt, mother refuses
Purpose: Sharpens Jay's worth-anxiety — his homemade gift weighed against Leo's globe-spanning one — the doubt the gala's payoff exists to answer.

What happens: First week of September: Amy and Luc return; **Tia Larson starts** (ex-military, blunt, capable — nothing like Gal, though Jay stays wary). She integrates fast; Friday brings her loud **welcome drinks** (she out-drinks Max and Luc). Ian leaves early, privately annoyed it cut into their Friday dinner. Two weeks to the gala.
Characters: Ian, Jay, Amy, Luc, Max, Gal, Tia
Milestone: Tia Larson joins the team (Gal's replacement)
Keywords: Tia starts, ex-military and blunt, welcome drinks, Ian leaves early (privately annoyed)
Purpose: Replaces Gal with her opposite so the office threat recedes — and shows Ian's quiet possessiveness over their Friday ritual.

What happens: Worn down, Jay relents: **Ema may attend** (with her best friend **Lam**), under strict conditions. Monday he visits **Hathaway's**, where the clerk **Eli** styles a bold floral-blazer look and offers a day-of hairstylist; Jay impulsively promises Eli an invitation. Ian, hearing Jay invited "an acquaintance," is quietly, pettily jealous.
Milestone: Ema (and Lam) cleared to attend; Eli styles Jay's gala look
Keywords: bold floral blazer, Eli the clerk, day-of hairstylist, Ema cleared, Ian pettily jealous
Purpose: Plants Ian's first overt jealousy (over Eli) and Jay's transformation into someone who can hold the gala's stage.

What happens: Jay secures Eli's invitation from Leo and recruits Ian's contacts to help find Tim's car. Leo, snooping in Jay's tincture-filled kitchen, teases that Jay clearly adores Ian. The final week before the gala closes in; Jay chooses his three best tinctures and boxes them with a handwritten note.
Keywords: Eli's invitation secured, Tim's car hunt, boxing the three best tinctures, the handwritten note
Purpose: Loads the gala's emotional payload — the boxed tinctures and the note — and lets Leo again name the thing Jay won't.

Jay, terrified but resolved to "live up to it," spends the night orbiting Ian, guarding Ema and Lam (Rus and Van on sentry duty), and shepherding the team.

What happens: Fireworks, a four-tier cake, a vaudeville star, a full orchestra. One of Ian's old friends mistakes Jay for Ian's "boyfriend"; both deny it, and Jay deflects every advance with a firm no. Ian aches to claim him and can't. Jay introduces Eli to Ian; at 2 a.m. he takes the exhausted, exhilarated girls home.

What happens: Home at 4 a.m., Ian ignores the mountain of gifts and goes straight to Jay's — three crystal bottles and a note ("You deserve every good thing"). He tastes the red-currant tincture and thinks *it probably tastes like his lips.* Leo visits to needle him; Ian, territorial, won't share a drop. Meanwhile Jay frets that he turned Ian's birthday into an exhausting spectacle.

What happens: Jay apologizes for the gala's excess; Ian absolves him and admits his living room is now "a warehouse" of unwanted gifts. Jay offers to help sort them Saturday. The **Tuesday runs are paused until spring** (autumn dark and cold, reporting season looming).

What happens: An afternoon sorting gifts on Ian's floor — domestic and intimate. Standouts: a two-person **sauna voucher** (from Rus and the gym crew), a "matching" gym hoodie, **botanical-garden night tickets** from Ema and Lam (a spring date Ian is openly delighted by), and Leo's absurd gem-encrusted **brooch** (which Jay laughs himself sick over). They agree to a **November vacation together**, framed as "administrative work."

What happens: **Q4 quarterly reporting** begins — and, with Ray and Liv at full power, finishes early with **no overtime** for the first time ever. Evenings become trip-planning sessions; they book the **Songbird Islands** (a cliff-side suite, ten days, first half of November). Leo announces the **hot-springs prize trip for the last weekend of October**. A Friday regulatory emergency costs them a Friday dinner.

Jay arrives to a warm family welcome; **Saturday is Tim's eighteenth birthday**, and the car reveal overwhelms everyone (even Jay's stepfather thaws). Ema admits Leo steered the botanical-garden gift. Jay trains home Sunday, leaving the car with Tim.

What happens: Back at work, the team clears the decks before the trip. Jay shops for winter gear (a ridiculous earflap hat). **Friday morning** they fly to **Ashland** (two hours north); even **Ray comes** ("I love hot springs"). At cliff-side **Frostbay Sanctuary**, rooms are paired — Ian with Jay.

Keywords: winter gear, the ridiculous earflap hat, even Ray comes, paired rooms (Ian with Jay)

What happens: After hours soaking, a freezing walk, and a hearty floor-cushion dinner, the **aurora** appears over an ocean-edge hot pool — the most beautiful thing Jay has ever seen. Back in the suite, wine and heat put Jay to sleep mid-sentence; Ian covers him and realizes he may never have been this happy.

Weather: freezing clear night, aurora over an ocean-edge hot pool
Keywords: the aurora, floor-cushion dinner, wine and heat, "may never have been this happy"

What happens: A morning hike goes wrong — Jay, in only a sweater (no thermal base layers), nearly freezes and Ian marches him back. The afternoon recovers on a heated terrace. Then a **communal northern-style (nude) sauna** — Ray's unprecedented enthusiasm makes it unmissable — leaves Jay staring fixedly at "very interesting wood," helplessly aware of Ian beside him.

Weather: dangerous cold; recovering on a heated terrace
Keywords: near-freeze (no base layers), Ian marches him back, nude northern sauna, "very interesting wood"

What happens: Rattled by the sauna, Jay over-drinks at dinner; Ian walks him out into the cold. Clinging to Ian's warmth, Jay whispers **"I want you to hold me for the rest of my life… just tonight, please."** Ian holds him to sleep and, into the dark, answers what Jay won't remember: **"I would. God help me, I would."**

Keywords: over-drinking, "hold me for the rest of my life… just tonight", "I would. God help me, I would.", the unremembered answer

What happens: Jay wakes mortified and half-remembering; Ian, hollowed out, insists "nothing happened" and blames a migraine. They massage, soak, and fly home in fraught quiet. Alone at last, Ian lets himself fall apart — Jay said exactly what Ian most wanted to hear, and that is the cruellest part.

Keywords: "nothing happened", the migraine excuse, fraught quiet, Ian falls apart alone

What happens: A punishing pre-vacation week of handovers (Ian setting a relentless pace, possibly to avoid thinking). Over a Saturday lunch he tells Jay plainly to stop treating their friendship "as something you need to earn back," then they shop for beach clothes and factor-fifty sunscreen.
Milestone: Ian dispels the post-retreat tension
Keywords: punishing handover pace, "stop treating it as something you need to earn back", factor-fifty sunscreen

What happens: They fly to the **Songbird Islands** — a palace-like cliff resort, a two-bedroom suite over the sea, **ten days**. At the bar a woman flirts with Jay; Ian smoothly intercepts (inventing dinner reservations). The first of many rescues, and the first of the trip's running themes: Jay is the most beautiful person in every room and the only one who doesn't know it.

What happens: Slow mornings with coffee on the terrace; Ian swims punishing laps while Jay floats. A pushy man corners Jay by the pool; he extracts himself, and Ian gently reframes it — Jay's warmth isn't a flaw, the harassment isn't his fault. Jay sulks pointedly while Ian does an hour of email.
Keywords: a pushy man by the pool, "your warmth isn't a flaw", pointed sulking, Ian's hour of email

What happens: At the resort's most romantic restaurant, the two trade their full romantic histories: **Ian's** (out to himself at fifteen, a conditional father, a volatile fling, a year-long relationship that failed on his unavailability, a managed-distraction phase, then Sam, then seven years alone) and **Jay's** (a one-night non-event, a two-month relationship that ended with "not interesting enough," the first-job harassment, then nothing).

Keywords: Ian's full history (a conditional father, Sam, seven years alone), Jay's (a non-event, "not interesting enough", the harassment)

What happens: The fourth evening. The persistent man returns; Ian declares "Jay is not available," then calls Jay **beautiful** (the second time ever) — and Jay short-circuits. Worn down by a week of Jay's nearness he can't claim, Ian drinks himself into a silent, total shutdown. **Jay rescues him for once**, putting him to bed and tending his hangover the next day. Ian, disarmed, promises the sunset cruise.
Milestone: Ian's breaking point; the roles reverse
Keywords: "Jay is not available", "beautiful" (the second time ever), Ian drinks to a shutdown, Jay rescues him

What happens: To deter the persistent man, Ian proposes they **"play into the assumption" that they're a couple.** At breakfast, watched, Ian lifts Jay's hand and **kisses his knuckles** — "this should do it" — and it lands on both of them like a first kiss. The pretence is on, and the touches accrue all day. That evening, a classical concert (where Ian apologizes for the earlier Sam-haunted one and promises to **play violin** for Jay at home).

Keywords: "play into the assumption", the knuckle kiss, "this should do it", the violin promise, the concert apology

What happens: A gaudy two-day cruise Ian openly loathes and Jay defends. They talk fatherhood and inheritance (Ian's fear of becoming his father; Jay's absent biological father), and a tipsy "committing to the bit" slow dance becomes a real, lingering embrace. No dolphins, no bioluminescence; Ian is vindicated and unbothered.

Keywords: the loathed cruise, fathers and inheritance, "committing to the bit", a slow dance that becomes real

What happens: A storm confines them indoors, and the day turns quietly domestic — planning a spring **Brussels/Geneva** work trip, debating a dark-green rug, Jay's mortgage. Ian states it plainly: he isn't here out of obligation — "I wanted to spend ten days with you… This is what I want." Jay answers that he doesn't want it to end.

What happens: A near-kiss in the suite doorway dissolves into **holding hands** as they walk into a day of deliberately doing nothing — pool, beach, a long barefoot walk in the surf, a shared sweater that smells of Ian. Jay has never been happier.

What happens: Back to a recycled-air office and a four-digit inbox, Jay grieves the trip for days and hates himself for it. **Thursday** Ian closes his laptop at six and drives Jay to **the Palm House** — a hidden Victorian glasshouse (Ian's own refuge the winter after his family cut him off). What happens: Ian offers his **home gym** for the winter (keeping their Tuesdays alive). What happens: Ian appears at 7 a.m. with soup, lemons, and a medical-grade thermometer for a cold that doesn't exist. Friday dinner is Jay's choice (a roast hall in the industrial east).
